chunk:
CHUNK:016/021
\u201cOf course, he was Barry back then, flying under the name of Barry August. He always said you needed something easy for an alias, to make it natural, easier to remember, and convincing. Well, I was convinced. About Sean, I mean.\u201d Rachael wandered along the middle of the longhouse, towards a small modern table at the far end. I followed, content to let her keep going, as she pulled me into her story. \u201cThis was only a few years ago. We needed to get out of South America, and we wanted to go to Europe. But that didn\u2019t quite work out, and we almost got ourselves pinned in London. We needed to take some time out, somewhere distant, and off the beaten track.\u201d \u201cGreenland,\u201d I said. \u201cThat\u2019s right, honey,\u201d Rachael said. \u201cYou remember all those reasons you gave me about Greenland being the lousiest place in which to plan an escape? Well, you ain\u2019t wrong, but if you\u2019re not looking to escape, then everything you mentioned turns out to be just perfect when you want to lose yourself in plain sight. Right?\u201d \u201cI suppose so,\u201d I said, frowning again as I tried to follow Rachael\u2019s train of thought. \u201cOf course, all this quiet, and all this nature with your big open skies, glaciers, and such\u2026 Well, it can be all kinds of inspirational, and Sean, dear Sean, was inspired.\u201d Rachael stopped at the table at the end of the longhouse, put the MP5 down, and leafed through the pages of the guestbook, going backwards from the last entries, turning back time as she moved through the years. \u201cSean had this grand plan,\u201d she said, stopping at one page and running the tip of her red nail down the entries. \u201cHe was still peeved about London. When the buyer pulled out, he was left with a tonne of hot diamonds that we couldn\u2019t sell and couldn\u2019t get out of the country. You can\u2019t fly with a couple of million dollars\u2019 worth of diamonds in your luggage.\u201d Rachael looked at me and smiled. \u201cI don\u2019t suppose you\u2019ve tried, honey? Have you?\u201d She smiled again as I shook my head. \u201cBut what you can do is take them on a boat, especially if you pay top dollar. This is before they beefed up security on all the cruises. We chose the ones without the x-ray scanners for your luggage. They have them on most ships now, but the adventure cruises, back then, they were still taking off, and the kind of passengers booking them\u2026\u201d Rachael shrugged and said, \u201cLet\u2019s just say, they\u2019re not exactly America\u2019s Most Wanted, eh?\u201d \u201cI suppose not,\u201d I said, thinking of the stereotypical cruise ship passenger with enough money to pay for a cruise to Greenland, usually at the end of their career, having worked hard to put enough money aside for the pension and some to spare for the fun stuff, like ticking things off the bucket list, Greenland included. \u201cSo, with a bag full of diamonds, and no buyer, Sean needed to get them into the States. He came up with this great idea and made some friends among the passengers. Some of them travel in groups, you know, usually friends from one town or state who\u2019ve known each other for a couple of decades. Sean got to talking with them, told them he was in the jewellery business, and would they like to see some of the things he had with him?\u201d \u201cAnd they did,\u201d I said. \u201cOh, yes.\u201d Rachael laughed. \u201cEspecially the ladies. But more than one of the gents was impressed by the thought of wearing a diamond studded bracelet on their watch.\u201d \u201cAnd Sean gave them the jewellery?\u201d \u201cHe let them borrow the jewellery. The idea was the diamonds were on loan for the duration of the voyage. And so he parted out the diamonds from the stash, with the idea that when these fine and innocent folk flew back to the States, a diamond ring with matching earrings in the luggage of one passenger, and a bracelet in the luggage of another, wouldn\u2019t attract the attention of customs or those border patrol ruffians you often meet in America.\u201d Rachael paused, and I guessed she was speaking from experience. \u201cNow, Sean played it real cool. He never asked for any kind of insurance, didn\u2019t take names or email addresses, but he made sure we were on all the excursions. And he made sure he was the first of our group to write in the guestbook, setting a precedent, you know?\u201d \u201cI think so,\u201d I said, although I wasn\u2019t sure. \u201cHe would joke when the passengers jostled him to hurry up. They\u2019d tease him and ask if he was writing a novel or something? Tell him things like for gawd\u2019s sake, Sean, it\u2019s not poetry.\u201d Rachael smiled and said, \u201cI liked that one.\u201d She flipped to another page in the guestbook and ran her finger down another column of names. \u201cAnother fellow said he thought Sean must be writing Pulitzer material, and that he was probably a journalist. But you know what Sean said?\u201d Rachael turned to look at me, pupils dilated in the dim light. \u201cHe told them if a thing was worth doing it was worth doing well, and that after the fine folks in Greenland had taken the time to build such a captivating \u2013 he liked that word \u2013 reconstruction of a piece of important history, the very least he could do was print his damned name so it was legible, and tell them where he had come from, because they would surely like to know.\u201d Rachael paused again, and I prompted her to continue with a nod. \u201cBut he wasn\u2019t done. He told them they should at least put their mail address in the book, telling them some story about the Viking ancestors sending them a Christmas card each December, or some such rubbish. But they bought it, of course.
compressed_context:
H1: DIAMOND-SMUGGLING  
CTX: LONGHOUSE-CONVERSATION  
  ACTORS: RACHEAL ∧ NARRATOR  
  PAST: RACHEAL WANDERS MIDDLE→TABLE-END ∧ SETS MP5 DOWN  
PAST: SEAN=ALIAS BARRY-AUGUST (EASY ∧ NATURAL ∧ MEMORABLE ∧ CONVINCING)  
PAST: ROUTE:SOUTH-AMERICA→EUROPE FAILED ∧ NEAR-PINNED IN LONDON  
PAST: CHOSE GREENLAND (QUIET ∧ OPEN-SKIES ∧ GLACIERS) ∧ SECLUSION  
CTX: DIAMOND-STASH  
  PAST: LONDON-BUYER PULL-OUT ∴ STASH=1 TONNE HOT-DIAMONDS≈USD2M UNSOLD  
  FEASIBILITY: AIR-TRANSPORT↛POSSIBLE  
PAST: SELECTED ADVENTURE-CRUISES WITHOUT X-RAY-SCANNERS FOR LUGGAGE  
TARGET: RETIREE-GROUP-PASSENGERS  
ACTION: SEAN OFFERS JEWELLERY-LOAN DURING VOYAGE  
DISTRIBUTION: RING+EARRINGS→PASSENGER-A LUGGAGE ∧ BRACELET→PASSENGER-B  
RESULT: DIAMONDS HIDDEN ∧ CUSTOMS-INSPECTION↛DETECTION  
CTX: GUESTBOOK-TACTIC  
  SEAN: WRITE-FIRST ENTRY ∧ SET PRECEDENT  
  PASSENGERS: ASSUME AUTHOR=JOURNALIST ∧ PULITZER-MATERIAL  
  SEAN: INSTRUCT PASSENGERS TO RECORD MAIL-ADDRESS  
  SEAN: STORY=VIKING-ANCESTORS CHRISTMAS-CARD  
  PASSENGERS: COMPLY  
OUTCOME: MAIL-ADDRESSES LOGGED ∧ OPERATION SUCCESSFUL
